original_abstract:
The central theme of Sirach 6:18–37 is the pursuit and acquisition of wisdom. This pericope is structured into three distinct movements: verses 18–22 outline the prerequisites for wisdom formation; verses 23–31 discuss the necessity of submission to wisdom’s authority; and the final section, verses 32–37, details the practical means by which wisdom is obtained. This article provides a focused analysis of the first segment (vv. 18–22), which can be further divided into two thematic sections.

In the first section (vv. 18–19), the sage employs agricultural metaphors to exhort the disciple toward patient labor. The author presents the acquisition of wisdom as a lifelong discipline, emphasizing that while the process requires enduring effort, those who commit to this formation will soon enjoy its rich harvest. The second section (vv. 20–22) contrasts this with a portrait of the "uneducated" individual who lacks the intellectual and moral fortitude—literally translated as "heart"—to sustain such a pursuit. Lacking patience, this person finds the demands of wisdom burdensome and ultimately rejects it. The author concludes that while wisdom is accessible to anyone regardless of social standing or origin, it remains the domain of a select few. The deciding factor is not external status, but an internal disposition characterized by a willingness to suffer effort and exercise the patience required to become truly wise.
humanized_abstract:
Wisdom of discernment is the central theme of Sir 6:18–37. The structure of the pericope presents three developments, each of four verses: 18–22 provide the necessary premises for coming to acquire wisdom, 23–31 expound the importance of recognizing the dominion of wisdom, and 32–37 describe the way to attain wisdom. This exegesis concentrates on the first part, that is, on the constituent elements of the initial developing movement of the text and focuses especially on the two subsections into which it can be divided.

The first section of the passage (vs. 18-19) uses images and language from the agricultural world to entreat the student to bear the hard labor that is required for acquiring wisdom. The teacher notes that this labor is very hard, but that those who persevere and suffer the hard work will find that there is a great fruit that they can reap from their labor and acquire wisdom. In the second section (vs. 20-22) an "uneducated person" is contrasted with the student described in the first section of the passage and shown to be incapable of having the strong heart that is needed to undergo the struggle involved in acquiring wisdom. The uneducated person is too lacking in patience to tolerate the difficulties that accompany the quest for wisdom and so turns back and gives up attempting to acquire wisdom. The teacher points out to the student to the world outside the school, and observes that wisdom is readily available to all who care to acquire it, but that very few are able to be wise. In this case, it is not the student's social status or origins that determine whether or not they can be wise, but whether or not the student has the type of inner makeup which is able to withstand the suffering that comes from the hard work that is required for wisdom and is thus able to bear the patience that is needed to acquire wisdom.
